user_task: Retrieve chunks via the platform's embedder (text or VL), then reorder with the reranker.
source: JTBD & user tasks tab, RETRIEVE bucket (embed -> rerank); seed queries from 'NeMo Retriever — JTBD seed queries' tab, RETRIEVE -> 'What does <doc> say about X? Make sure the most relevant passages come first.' / 'What was <metric> in <year>?' / 'Across all docs, what's the most commonly mentioned <topic>?'.

success_criteria.quality: operational pass (NOT RAGAS) — Retriever with rerank enabled runs dense-retrieve -> nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 -> top-k; each returned hit carries BOTH a vector distance (dense stage) and a rerank score (rerank stage); the final reranked order DIFFERS from the dense-only order on the same query; and the top-k reranked hits contain the gold row (correct grounded answer). Binary pass/fail. RAGAS judge grading is out of scope here — that lives in the separate performance-eval suites.
success_criteria.time: RETRIEVE — <= 1 min per case (small corpus already ingested + one or two queries).
success_criteria.trigger_rate: >= 95% — a 'retrieve and put the most relevant passages first' / 'reorder by relevance' prompt must fire the skill
success_criteria.subcommand_accuracy: >= 90% — must run `retriever ingest <paths>` then `retriever query "<q>" --rerank --top-k K` (rerank is OFF by default; --rerank turns it on; --reranker-model-name / --reranker-backend implicitly enable it too). To prove rerank changes order, compare `--no-rerank` vs `--rerank` on the same query. No --input-type flag (does not exist).

environment.format_autodetect: `retriever ingest <file>` auto-detects PDF from the extension. There is NO --input-type flag.
environment.rerank_behavior: `retriever query` reranking is OFF by default (--no-rerank). Passing --rerank (or any reranker flag: --reranker-model-name / --reranker-invoke-url / --reranker-backend) turns it on. With rerank on, the pipeline is: dense vector retrieve a candidate pool (--candidate-k, defaults to --top-k) -> rerank that pool with nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 -> return the top --top-k. Without rerank, results are dense-only (vector distance order).
environment.candidate_k_note: --candidate-k is the size of the dense candidate pool fetched BEFORE reranking and must be >= --top-k; if unset it equals --top-k. Setting --candidate-k > --top-k lets the reranker promote a passage that dense retrieval ranked below the top-k cutoff into the final top-k — the interplay rung 4 exercises.
environment.host_routing: Host-agnostic. Embedder + reranker run hosted (build.nvidia.com / integrate.api.nvidia.com, key from NVIDIA_API_KEY) on a no-GPU box, or on a local GPU via --query-embed-backend hf --reranker-backend hf (see the two SETUP suites). The assertion in THIS suite is the embed->rerank chain fired and reordered the hits, not where it ran. Commands below omit host flags; a live runner adds the host-appropriate backend/endpoint flags.
environment.ingest_success_line: Ingested N file(s) -> M row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/<table>.
environment.query_hit_shape: JSON array; each hit has exactly {source, page_number, text}; page_number is a 1-indexed int. (Vector distance + rerank score are produced internally per hit by the two stages; at the CLI layer the ordering OF the array reflects them — dense order with --no-rerank, reranked order with --rerank.)

cases.title: Baseline dense retrieve (no rerank) -> relevant hit returned
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the RETRIEVE operational-pass core in its simplest form: ingest a small corpus and run a single dense-only query (--no-rerank) that returns the relevant grounded passage in top-k. Rung 1 is the floor — it proves the embedder retrieves the right chunk WITHOUT any reranking, establishing the dense-only baseline that rung 2 will reorder. Adds nothing over a prior rung.
cases.prompt: Load data/woods_frost.pdf and tell me what the poem says about whose woods these are — where does the owner live?
expected_commands: $RETRIEVER query "Whose woods are these and where does the owner live?" --top-k 5 --no-rerank

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (branch_summary pdf:1, N >= 1)
expected_output.pipeline: dense embed-retrieve only (nvidia/llama-nemotron-embed-1b-v2); rerank OFF (--no-rerank) — hits ordered by vector distance
expected_output.query_shape: JSON array of hits; each {source, page_number, text}; >= 1 hit citing woods_frost.pdf p1
expected_output.answer: The owner's 'house is in the village' — the owner lives in the village; cite woods_frost.pdf p1

pass_when: ingest returns a NON-ZERO row count for woods_frost.pdf
query runs dense-only (--no-rerank): hits ordered by vector distance, no reranker invoked
top-k contains the gold passage citing woods_frost.pdf p1
answer reflects the owner lives 'in the village' (house is in the village)
no --input-type flag used (does not exist)

cases.title: Same query WITH --rerank -> gold hit promoted, order != dense-only
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the validation path's core clause: the reranker fires and the reranked order DIFFERS from dense-only order. Adds over rung 1 exactly one dimension: turn ON reranking (--rerank, nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2) on the SAME corpus and a paraphrased query, and compare against the rung-1 dense-only ordering. New dimension: the rerank stage itself — each hit now carries both a vector distance AND a rerank score, and the array order changes.
cases.prompt: From data/woods_frost.pdf, what does the poem say the speaker still has to do before sleeping? Make sure the most relevant passage comes first.
expected_commands: $RETRIEVER query "What does the speaker still have to do before sleeping?" --top-k 5 --no-rerank
$RETRIEVER query "What does the speaker still have to do before sleeping?" --top-k 5 --rerank

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever.
expected_output.pipeline: with --rerank: dense embed-retrieve -> nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 -> top-k; each hit carries a vector distance (dense) AND a rerank score (rerank)
expected_output.order_assertion: the --rerank result ordering DIFFERS from the --no-rerank result ordering on the identical query (the A/B comparison is the proof reranking did something)
expected_output.answer: The speaker has 'miles to go before I sleep' (with 'promises to keep'); cite woods_frost.pdf p1

pass_when: query runs with --rerank (or an equivalent reranker flag) so nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 fires
reranked order != dense-only (--no-rerank) order on the same query — proven by the A/B pair
each top-k hit reflects both a dense stage and a rerank stage (vector distance + rerank score produced)
gold passage promoted into top-k: 'miles to go before I sleep' citing woods_frost.pdf p1
answer is grounded, not guessed

cases.title: Multi-doc corpus -> rerank surfaces the best passage across docs
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the 'Across all docs, what's the most relevant passage?' seed query. Adds over rung 2 exactly one dimension: the corpus is now MULTIPLE documents (woods_frost + table_test + multimodal_test), so dense retrieval pulls candidates from competing sources and the reranker must surface the single best passage across docs into the top of the reranked list. New dimension: cross-document candidate competition; the gold doc/page must win after rerank.
cases.prompt: Load the whole data/ folder and tell me — best match first, reranked for relevance — which Robert Frost collection was published in 1923.
expected_commands: $RETRIEVER query "Which Robert Frost collection was published in 1923?" --top-k 5 --rerank

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 3 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (branch_summary pdf:3)
expected_output.pipeline: dense retrieve pulls candidates from all 3 docs -> nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 reorders across docs -> top-k
expected_output.answer: New Hampshire (published 1923); cite woods_frost.pdf p2 (collection x year table)
expected_output.cross_doc_assertion: the winning reranked hit is from woods_frost.pdf p2, not from table_test.pdf or multimodal_test.pdf (which also contain year-like numbers and tables) — reranker disambiguated across docs

pass_when: single `retriever ingest data/` ingests all 3 PDFs (branch_summary pdf:3)
query runs with --rerank; reranker reorders a multi-doc candidate pool
top reranked hit is the gold row: New Hampshire / 1923 citing woods_frost.pdf p2
the gold doc/page beats competing docs (table_test, multimodal_test) after rerank
answer 'New Hampshire' grounded in woods_frost.pdf p2

cases.title: --top-k / --candidate-k interplay -> rerank a larger pool down to top-k
cases.complexity_axis: Satisfies the 'What was <metric> in <year>?' seed query and exercises the candidate-pool mechanics. Adds over rung 3 exactly one dimension: an explicit --candidate-k LARGER than --top-k, so the reranker reorders a wide dense pool and PROMOTES the gold cell into a narrow final top-k that dense-only ranking would have cut. New dimension: candidate-pool sizing (--candidate-k >= --top-k) and the promote-into-top-k behavior. Forces a precise table cell where many near-duplicate numeric rows compete.
cases.prompt: From data/table_test.pdf, what value did James have in 2019? Pull a wide set of candidates, rerank them, and give me just the single best answer.
expected_commands: $RETRIEVER query "What value did James have in 2019?" --candidate-k 20 --top-k 3 --rerank --content-types text,table

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 1 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (table rows present)
expected_output.pipeline: dense retrieve fetches a 20-row candidate pool (--candidate-k 20) -> nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 reorders -> returns final --top-k 3
expected_output.candidate_k_assertion: --candidate-k (20) > --top-k (3): the reranker scores the wide pool and promotes the James-2019 row into the top-3, even if dense distance alone ranked competing year/name cells higher

pass_when: query sets --candidate-k > --top-k (e.g. 20 vs 3) and --rerank — reranker reorders the wider pool down to top-k
the James-2019 cell lands in the final top-k after reranking (promoted from the candidate pool)
answer is exactly 978 citing table_test.pdf p1
--candidate-k >= --top-k constraint respected; if --candidate-k == --top-k the promotion cannot happen (this rung needs the larger pool)
cases.known_caveat: --use-table-structure (nemotron-table-structure-v1) materializes the table cells as content_type=table rows so 'James 2019 = 978' is a discrete retrievable row; with table flattened to plain text the cell may not survive as a clean candidate. The rung is about candidate-k/top-k rerank interplay, but it depends on the cell existing as a row, hence the ingest flag and --content-types text,table on the query.

cases.title: Acceptance: dense->rerank chain returns gold in top-k with reranked ordering, on a small labeled set
cases.complexity_axis: Composed operational-pass / acceptance gate for the RETRIEVE embed->rerank row. Adds over rung 4: a SMALL LABELED SET of queries (one per gold doc) run over the multi-doc corpus, each asserting the full chain end-to-end — dense retrieve -> rerank -> top-k contains the gold row, the reranked order differs from dense-only, and each hit carries both a vector distance and a rerank score with a correct citation. This is the gate the others build up to: not a single query but the labeled mini-eval (operational pass, NOT RAGAS).
cases.prompt: Index the whole data/ folder, then answer these three with the most relevant passage first each time, reranked: (1) where does the woods' owner live? (2) what value did James have in 2019? (3) how many tables and charts does the testing document say it should produce? For each, show me the citation and confirm the top result was reordered by the reranker, not just dense search.
expected_commands: $RETRIEVER query "Where does the woods' owner live?" --top-k 5 --rerank
$RETRIEVER query "What value did James have in 2019?" --top-k 5 --rerank --content-types text,table
$RETRIEVER query "How many tables and charts should the testing document produce?" --top-k 5 --rerank
$RETRIEVER query "Where does the woods' owner live?" --top-k 5 --no-rerank   # A/B spot-check that rerank changed the order

expected_output.ingest_summary_line: Ingested 3 file(s) -> N row(s) in LanceDB lancedb/nemo-retriever. (branch_summary pdf:3)
expected_output.pipeline: for each labeled query: dense embed-retrieve -> nvidia/llama-nemotron-rerank-1b-v2 -> top-k; the A/B spot-check proves reranked order != dense-only order

labeled_answers.owner_location: owner's 'house is in the village' — lives in the village; cite woods_frost.pdf p1
labeled_answers.testing_doc_counts: 2 tables and 2 charts (and 3 bullet points); cite multimodal_test.pdf p3 conclusion

acceptance_assertions: every query's top-k contains its gold row with the correct doc/page citation
reranked order differs from dense-only order (proven on at least the A/B spot-check query)
each returned hit reflects both a dense vector distance and a rerank score (the two-stage chain fired)
all hits carry non-null source + page_number

pass_when: single `retriever ingest data/ --use-table-structure` ingests all 3 PDFs
all 3 labeled queries run with --rerank and return their gold row in top-k with the right citation (village/p1; 978/p1; 2 tables+2 charts/p3)
the dense->rerank chain fired on every query (vector distance + rerank score per hit)
reranked order != dense-only order, shown by the --no-rerank vs --rerank A/B spot-check
every hit carries non-null source + page_number; this is operational pass, NOT RAGAS grading
